tldr: Moving from Nium's global payouts/issuing infrastructure to Yuno's multi-PSP checkout orchestration is less a migration than a re-platforming: the two products solve different problems, so plan an integration-side rebuild rather than a like-for-like swap.
why_switch: Yuno is positioned as merchant-side payment orchestration with multi-PSP routing, while Nium is infrastructure for cross-border payouts/issuing
If your real need is card acceptance and routing optimization, Yuno fits better than Nium
Yuno reports lower lock-in via its orchestration abstraction layer
Single API across PSPs can simplify checkout maintenance versus stitching together Nium plus separate PSPs
May reduce engineering surface area if you currently use Nium for use cases it was not designed for
what_to_export: List of payment methods and PSPs currently in use
Stored customer/payer records and their payment method tokens (where portable)
API credentials inventory and rotation plan
export_method: Use Nium's official APIs and merchant dashboard exports to pull transaction, customer, and balance data. Because Nium and Yuno serve different layers of the stack, treat the export primarily as a finance/reporting handoff rather than a data import. Coordinate with Nium account management for any bulk historical exports and confirm token portability for stored payment instruments — consult official docs for exact scopes and formats.
import_method: Yuno is implemented as a new orchestration layer in front of your PSPs. Provision a Yuno workspace, connect the PSPs you want routed (which may or may not include Nium for payouts), and rebuild checkout against Yuno's SDKs/APIs. Historical Nium data is generally kept in your warehouse for finance, not re-imported into Yuno. Follow Yuno's official onboarding documentation for current connector and SDK options.

concept_mapping.note: Yuno focuses on payment acceptance/orchestration, not cross-border payouts
Card issuing is out of Yuno's stated orchestration scope
Collections become PSP-specific connectors configured inside Yuno
FX is now governed by the underlying PSP rather than the orchestrator
Re-create on Yuno; verify field-level mapping in Yuno docs
Re-subscribe and re-implement handlers per Yuno's event schema

gotchas: Nium and Yuno are not feature-parity products — do not assume a one-to-one swap
If you rely on Nium for payouts or card issuing, Yuno will not replace those workflows
Stored card/token portability between providers is rarely automatic; expect re-tokenization or PCI-scoped migration via your PSP
Compliance and licensing footprints differ — re-verify which entity is the regulated party for each flow
Reconciliation reports change shape; finance teams need lead time to adapt
Run the old and new stacks in parallel during cutover to catch routing or webhook regressions

email.subject: Migration plan: Nium to Yuno
email.body:
Team,

Proposing we move our payments orchestration from Nium to Yuno. The two are not direct equivalents — Nium is global payouts/issuing infrastructure while Yuno is merchant-side multi-PSP orchestration — so this is more of a re-platforming than a swap.

Plan:
1. Inventory current Nium usage (payouts, issuing, collections) and identify which flows actually belong in Yuno.
2. Export transaction, customer, and reconciliation data from Nium for finance retention.
3. Stand up Yuno, connect required PSPs, rebuild checkout via Yuno SDKs.
4. Run both stacks in parallel during cutover; cap traffic on Yuno gradually.
5. Decommission Nium flows we no longer need; keep Nium for any payout/issuing flows Yuno does not cover.

Estimated effort: 3-8 weeks depending on how many Nium flows we still need separately. I'll confirm specifics against Nium and Yuno official docs before we kick off.

Thanks
